coredata.dc:title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of carbonized polymer dots/CdS quantum dots/Bi2WO6 Z-scheme heterojunctions: Enhancing photocatalytic environmental remediation via dual-quantum dot heterostructures

coredata.dc:description: The fabrication of dual-quantum dot heterostructures offers a promising strategy to enhance the environmental remediation performance of photocatalysts. Herein, a Bi2WO6-based Z-scheme heterojunction was constructed by incorporating carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) and CdS quantum dots (QDs) via a microwave-assisted solvothermal method. The 1 wt% CPDs/CdS QDs/Bi2WO6 (CCBW-1) composite achieved optimal Cr(VI) removal, reaching 97.7 % within 30 min under 10 W LED light, with rate constants 4.4, 2.8, and 10.1 times higher than those of pristine Bi2WO6, CdS QDs/Bi2WO6, and 3 % CPDs/Bi2WO6, respectively. Notably, the composite also demonstrated 96.9 % Cr(VI) and 98.1 % Rhodamine B (RhB) removal within 30 min in a mixed Cr(VI) and RhB solution. The formation of strong BiS and WOCd bonds at the Bi2WO6 CdS QD interface facilitates intimate interfacial contacts and creates atomic-scale “highways” that accelerate charge transfer. Additionally, the electron-donating effects of the NH2 and OH functional groups on the CPDs further enhance carrier transfer efficiency. The Z-scheme electron transport pathway enables CCBW-1 to capitalize on the deep reduction potentials and extended light absorption of the dual-QDs, allowing them to act synergistically as active centers. By integrating experimental data with theoretical calculations, the photocatalytic mechanism, potential intermediates, photodegradation pathway, and biological toxicity were comprehensively elucidated.

head.ce:title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of carbonized polymer dots/CdS quantum dots/Bi2WO6 Z-scheme heterojunctions: Enhancing photocatalytic environmental remediation via dual-quantum dot heterostructures

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel Z-scheme CPDs/CdS QDs/Bi2WO6 dual-quantum dot heterojunction was constructed via a facial microwave-induced method for achieving high-performance photocatalytic Cr(VI) and RhB removal simultaneously under a 10 W LED illumination.

The fabrication of dual-quantum dot heterostructures offers a promising strategy to enhance the environmental remediation performance of photocatalysts. Herein, a Bi2WO6-based Z-scheme heterojunction was constructed by incorporating carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) and CdS quantum dots (QDs) via a microwave-assisted solvothermal method. The 1 wt% CPDs/CdS QDs/Bi2WO6 (CCBW-1) composite achieved optimal Cr(VI) removal, reaching 97.7 % within 30 min under 10 W LED light, with rate constants 4.4, 2.8, and 10.1 times higher than those of pristine Bi2WO6, CdS QDs/Bi2WO6, and 3 % CPDs/Bi2WO6, respectively. Notably, the composite also demonstrated 96.9 % Cr(VI) and 98.1 % Rhodamine B (RhB) removal within 30 min in a mixed Cr(VI) and RhB solution. The formation of strong BiS and WOCd bonds at the Bi2WO6 CdS QD interface facilitates intimate interfacial contacts and creates atomic-scale “highways” that accelerate charge transfer. Additionally, the electron-donating effects of the NH2 and OH functional groups on the CPDs further enhance carrier transfer efficiency. The Z-scheme electron transport pathway enables CCBW-1 to capitalize on the deep reduction potentials and extended light absorption of the dual-QDs, allowing them to act synergistically as active centers. By integrating experimental data with theoretical calculations, the photocatalytic mechanism, potential intermediates, photodegradation pathway, and biological toxicity were comprehensively elucidated.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Carbonized polymer dots/CdS quantum dots/Bi2WO6 composite

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals and organic contaminants in industrial wastewater, particularly from electroplating and textile industries, persist in the water cycle, posing significant environmental hazards [1,2]. Among these, Cr(VI) pollution presents a global health threat, contributing to cancer and other serious diseases, and is classified as a primary carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer [3]. Consequently, there is an urgent need for effective, safe, and sustainable methods to eliminate Cr(VI) pollution. Photocatalytic technology meets these criteria and has gained considerable attention as a promising solution, not only for remediating polluted water [1] but also for a range of applications, including H2 evolution [4,5], CO2 reduction [6], H2O2 production [7], bacterial decontamination [8], and N2 fixation [9]. Among the photocatalysts explored, Aurivillius Bi-based semiconductors are well-known for their superior ability to eliminate heavy metal ions and organic pollutants in aqueous environments [10–12]. In particular, Bi2WO6 has garnered significant interest in photocatalysis due to its affordability, high stability, nontoxic nature, and remarkable photoactivity [13–15]. Moreover, Bi2WO6 tends to form an organized hierarchical structure, which shortens charge carrier migration paths at interfaces, increases reactive surface areas, and enhances light utilization, thereby boosting its photocatalytic efficiency [16,17]. However, achieving both broad-spectrum light absorption and strong photoexcited redox properties remains a challenge for pristine Bi2WO6 [18,19]. Additionally, its practical application is hindered by the slow kinetics of photogenerated charge separation and migration, as well as rapid recombination rates [20].

To address these challenges, the development of Z-scheme heterostructures by combining two semiconductors with compatible band structures has emerged as an effective strategy [21–23]. This approach modifies charge migration pathways, facilitating the spatial separation of electrons (e −) and holes (h +) within distinct regions of the heterostructure [3]. It also generates an internal electric field (IEF) and induces energy band bending, both of which reduce carrier recombination [24]. Several Bi2WO6-based Z-scheme heterojunctions, such as BiOCl/Bi2WO6 [25], g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 [26], In2S3/Bi2WO6 [27], TiO2/Bi2WO6 [28], and CdS/Bi2WO6 [29], have been developed to enhance the photocatalytic removal of pollutants. However, these efforts continue to face challenges such as insufficient atomic-level interfacial contact, high charge transfer resistance, and elevated exciton binding energy [10,24,26,28,29]. More critically, random charge carrier migration within the bulk materials often leads to undesirable recombination, due to long transport distances and internal defects that act as trapping and recombination centers [30,31]. As a result, achieving strongly coupled heterointerfaces with minimized migration distances from the bulk exciton regions to the interfaces remains a key challenge for efficient, low-loss charge transfer.

Quantum dots (QDs, <10 nm) offer a solution to the aforementioned challenges due to their nanoscale size, uniform distribution, and ability to establish strong interfacial contacts [32–34]. As a result, constructing QD heterostructures (QDHs) with properly aligned band energies provides a more efficient strategy for achieving atomic-level interfacial coupling compared to traditional heterostructures [35]. Several studies have demonstrated the successful synthesis of QDHs by incorporating QDs into bulk matrices, such as polymers and perovskites, which enhances both catalytic activity and recyclability [36–38]. Common semiconductor QDs used in Z-scheme Bi2WO6-based QDHs include CdS [39,40], CdSe [41], and CsPbBr3 [42]. Notably, CdS QDs are compatible with Bi2WO6 due to their negative reduction potential and strong ultraviolet and visible-light absorption (λ < 520 nm) [39,43]. For example, Kadeer et al. [39] synthesized a hierarchical CdS QDs/Bi2WO6 composite that achieved 94.5 % RhB degradation within 30 min, 1.5 times higher than pristine Bi2WO6. Similarly, Ge et al. [40] reported 97.0 % phenol degradation in 3 h using CdS QDs/Bi2WO6, a fivefold improvement over pristine Bi2WO6. These performance enhancements result from strong interfacial bonding and well-matched bandgap potentials. However, fabricating Z-scheme CdS QDs/Bi2WO6 heterostructures presents challenges, as CdS QDs are prone to photocorrosion under prolonged light exposure, leading to their decomposition into Cd2+ and S2− ions, which can cause secondary water pollution [44–46]. Additionally, conventional QDs face significant challenges in terms of tunable sizes and surface structures, leading to poor dispersion of CdS QDs/Bi2WO6 heterostructures in aqueous environments. Therefore, further optimization of the CdS QDs/Bi2WO6 Z-scheme QDH structure is essential for improving pollutant removal efficiency in photocatalytic processes.

Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) are a novel class of quasi-spherical carbon nanomaterials with diameters under 10 nm and abundant surface functional groups [47]. These unique properties—high biocompatibility, low toxicity, broad-spectrum light absorption, and polydispersity—have made CPDs highly versatile in applications such as photocatalysis [48], sensors [49], and photovoltaic devices [50]. As an inexpensive, metal-free alternative to conventional QDs, CPDs have gained considerable attention in photocatalyst design due to their quantum confinement and edge effects, upconverted photoluminescence (UCPL), and superior electron-donor/acceptor and transport properties [51–53]. These characteristics make CPDs particularly promising for enhancing photocatalytic efficiency [54–56]. Compared to traditional semiconductor or metal QDs, CPD-integrated QDHs offer several advantages, including expanded active interfaces, reduced phototoxicity, and tunable surface properties [14,15,57–60]. Furthermore, CPDs can enhance interfacial binding forces at semiconductor heterointerfaces through their electron-donor/acceptor effects, as well as significantly broaden the light-harvesting range. However, the photocatalytic efficiency of CPDs/Bi2WO6 QDHs is limited by the intrinsic positive conduction band potential of Bi2WO6, weak binding interactions between CPDs and Bi2WO6, and relatively low CPDs content in the heterostructure. In contrast, heterostructures based on traditional semiconductor QDs are more effective at enhancing redox activity and improving charge separation/migration kinetics. Therefore, developing dual-QD heterojunctions (DQDHs) is a promising strategy to combine the strengths of both traditional and metal-free QDs for improved photocatalytic performance. However, the fabrication of DQDH photocatalysts remains unexplored [31,35,61]. Hence, comprehensive studies are necessary to uncover the synergistic mechanisms of dual QDs in Z-scheme heterostructures.

In this study, a CPDs/CdS QDs/Bi2WO6 (CCBW) dual-QD heterostructure was synthesized using a microwave-assisted solvothermal method. The structural, morphological, optical, and electronic properties of the materials were thoroughly characterized. The photocatalytic performance of the optimized CCBW-1 was evaluated by measuring the removal of Cr(VI) and RhB. To gain deeper insights into interfacial interactions, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were employed. Notably, WOCd and BiS bonds formed at the heterointerface between CdS QDs and Bi2WO6, creating more intimate contacts and facilitating an atomic-level pathway for enhanced interfacial charge transfer. Additionally, the increased charge density on the Bi2WO6 side indicated strong interfacial interactions, primarily influenced by the NH2 and OH groups of the CPDs. The Z-scheme electron transfer mechanism within the heterojunction allows the dual QDs to function as two active centers for photocatalytic reactions. This work provides new insights into the use of DQDHs for wastewater pollutant removal under broad-spectrum solar irradiation.

Bismuth nitrate hydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, 99.0 %), sodium tungstate dihydrate (Na2WO4·2H2O, 99.5 %), and cadmium chloride dihydrate (CdCl2·2H2O, 99.0 %) were purchased from Tianjin Sailboat Chemical Reagent Technology Co., Ltd (China). Ethylene glycol (≥99.0 %) and absolute ethanol (≥99.0 %) were supplied by Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Technology Co., Ltd (China). Thiourea (99.0 %) and urea (99.0 %) were obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (China). 4-Hydroxy-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine N-oxide (TEMPOL, ≥97 %), 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine N-oxide (TEMPO, 98 %), and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO, ≥98.0 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. (USA). Nafion solution (5.0 %) was obtained from Suzhou Sinero Technology Co., Ltd. (China).

Synthesis of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs): CPDs were synthesized using a simple hydrothermal method, modified from previous studies [62]. Briefly, 335 μL of ethylenediamine and 1.05 g of citric acid were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. The solution was then transferred to an 80-mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 5 h. After cooling, the solution was dialyzed for 24 h. The final product was obtained by reduced-pressure distillation, followed by repeated washing with ethanol and water, and then dried at 60 °C for 8 h.

Synthesis of Bi2WO6 (BWO): Hierarchical Bi2WO6 was synthesized via a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method, as described in previous research by our group [63]. Specifically, 3 mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved in 4 mL of 36 % acetic acid (v/v) to form solution A, while 1.5 mmol of Na2WO4·2H2O was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water to form solution B. Solution A was gradually added to solution B under stirring, resulting in the formation of a precursor. The mixture was then transferred to a microwave reactor and heated at 160 °C for 40 min. The resulting solid was collected by filtration, thoroughly washed, and dried at 60 °C for 8 h to yield the final BWO product. A composite obtained by adding 3 wt% CPDs to the precursor was denoted as CPBW.

Construction of CPDs/CdS quantum dots (QDs)/Bi2WO6 (CCBW) heterojunction: The CPDs/CdS QDs/Bi2WO6 composite was synthesized via a microwave-assisted method, as illustrated in Scheme 1 . A specific amount of CPDs was dissolved in 20 mL of a 1:1 (v/v) mixed ethanol-ethylene glycol solution and sonicated for 10 min. Next, 1.5 mmol of Bi2WO6 was added to the solution and sonicated for an additional 20 min. Then, 0.75 mmol of CdCl2·2H2O, 0.171 g of thiourea, and 0.05 g of urea were sequentially added. The mixture was transferred to a microwave reactor (JUPITER-B, Shanghai Sineo Microwave Chemistry Co., Ltd., China) and heated at 160 °C for 40 min. The product was collected by filtration, washed thoroughly, and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The samples with CPD mass fractions of 1, 3, and 5 wt% were labeled as CCBW-1, CCBW-3, and CCBW-5, respectively. For comparison, the CdS QDs/Bi2WO6 (CBW) sample was synthesized without CPDs in the precursor mixture, using the same procedure. CdS QDs alone were also prepared by omitting both CPDs and Bi2WO6 from the precursor.
The characterization techniques are described in Text S1 (Supplementary Material).

The photocatalytic performance was evaluated by monitoring the removal of Cr(VI) and RhB under light exposure. A 10 W LED lamp, equipped with a 400-nm cutoff filter, was used as the light source, providing an optical power density of 450 mW/cm2. In each experiment, 50 mg of photocatalyst was dispersed in 50 mL of a 20-mg/L Cr(VI) solution to create a suspension. The mixture was stirred in the dark for 30 min in a multichannel photocatalytic reactor (PCX-50C Discover, Beijing PerfectLight Co. Ltd., China) to establish adsorption–desorption equilibrium. During light irradiation, 3-mL aliquots of the suspension were taken at predetermined intervals, centrifuged, and analyzed for absorbance at 540 nm using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer with the diphenylcarbazide method [64]. Similarly, RhB degradation was tested using a 20-mg/L solution, with absorbance monitored at 554 nm. The chemical stability of the photocatalysts was evaluated through multiple photocatalytic cycles. The removal efficiencies (η) of Cr(VI) and RhB were calculated using Eq. (1):

where C 0 is the initial concentration and Ct is the concentration at a specific time. The standard absorbance curve for the Cr(VI) solution is shown in Fig. S9.

DFT calculations were primarily performed using Materials Studio. The initial crystal models for Bi2WO6 and CdS QDs were obtained from the Materials Project database. For the Bi2WO6 (131) surfaces and (CdS)4 nanoclusters, these were chosen to construct monomers and a heterojunction, ensuring a vacuum thickness of 15 Å to eliminate interslab interactions. The CdS QDs/Bi2WO6 hybrid model is given in Fig. S1. Geometry optimizations were carried out using the Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof exchange–correlation functional under the generalized gradient approximation. Valence electrons were treated with a plane-wave basis set with a cutoff energy of 340.0 eV, along with ultrasoft pseudopotentials for core and valence electrons. Brillouin zone integration was performed using a 2 × 2 × 1 Monkhorst–Pack k-point grid. The convergence criteria for energy, maximum force, and maximum atomic displacement were set at 1 × 10−5 eV, 0.05 eV/Å, and 0.002 Å, respectively, with structural optimizations carried out using the Broyden–Fletcher–Goldfarb–Shanno method. For the CPD models, structures were constructed based on the graphene framework and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy data. The CPDs/Bi2WO6 hybrid model is shown in Fig. S2, and its geometry optimization was performed using the B3LYP/6-31+G** basis set in Gaussian 09 W software. CPD models, characterized by different functional groups, are shown in Fig. S3. The stability of the CPDs/Bi2WO6 models was evaluated through interfacial interaction energy (E int, eV) and adhesion work (W ad, J/m2), calculated using Eqs. (2) and (3):

where E total (eV) is the total energy of the heterostructure, E A and E B (eV) are the energies of components A and B of the heterostructure, respectively, and S (Å2) is the cross-sectional area of the system.

The morphology and microstructure of the obtained catalysts were characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). SEM images (Figs. 1a, S4a) and TEM images (Fig. 1 b) reveal that pristine Bi2WO6 (BWO) exhibits a hierarchical flower-like structure composed of two-dimensional (2D) nanosheets, each approximately 2–3 µm in diameter. This structure, featuring a large specific surface area and well-developed porous network, provides numerous active sites for photocatalysis while also shortening the migration paths for photogenerated charge carriers to the interface. As shown in Fig. 1c, CPDs display irregular morphologies, consisting of carbon nanodots with an average diameter of approximately 3 nm. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) (Fig. 1d) reveals a lattice space of 0.32 nm for CPDs, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of graphitic carbon [15]. In contrast, pristine CdS exhibits nanoparticle clusters with particle sizes ranging from 8 to 10 nm, characteristic of QDs. The lattice fringe spacing of 0.31 nm corresponds to the (101) plane of the hexagonal crystal phase (Fig. 1e, f). SEM imaging of the CdS QDs/Bi2WO6 (CBW) composite (Fig. 1g) shows that the hierarchical structure of BWO is preserved after integrating CdS QDs. Furthermore, the CBW composite displays distinct lattice fringes of 0.31 nm and 0.27 nm (Fig. 1h), corresponding to the CdS (101) and Bi2WO6 (2 0 0) crystal planes, respectively, confirming the close interfacial contact between the CdS QDs and BWO. Similarly, the CCBW-1 sample maintains the three-dimensional hierarchical morphology of pristine BWO after coassembly with both types of QDs (Fig. 1i). The HR-TEM image of CCBW-1 sample (Fig. 1j) shows hexagonal CdS QDs and disordered CPDs uniformly distributed on BWO nanosheets, indicating the successful fabrication of the DQDH. The overlapping lattice fringes observed in Fig. 1k further confirm the formation of a heterostructure with strong interfacial connections [65]. Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) mapping analysis (Fig. 1l, m) confirms the presence of Bi, W, O, Cd, S, and C in the CCBW-1 sample, which is consistent with the element spot analysis shown in Fig. S4b.

The specific surface area and pore distribution of the CPDs/CdS QDs/Bi2WO6 composite were evaluated using N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms. As shown in Fig. S5a, b, the isotherms of all samples exhibit typical type-IV adsorption curves with H3 hysteresis loops. The binary CBW heterojunction displays a larger specific surface area and smaller pore size compared to pristine BWO. This change can be attributed to the embedding of CdS QDs onto BWO nanosheets, which partially blocks their mesopores [63]. Consequently, a slight reduction in the absorption volume of CBW is observed in Fig. S5a. The increase in surface area indicates the presence of more active sites, which are beneficial for photocatalytic interfacial reactions. Upon the introduction of CPDs (∼3 nm), the pore size of the ternary CCBW-1 heterojunction is further reduced by approximately 0.1 nm. Although the specific surface area of CCBW-1 is slightly lower than that of CBW, its photocatalytic performance is expected to be enhanced due to the abundant surface functional groups and superior light-harvesting capability of CPDs.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted to confirm the crystal structure of the synthesized catalysts, as shown in Fig. 2 a. CPDs exhibit a broad peak at 2θ = 26.4°, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of graphitic carbon, as observed in the TEM images. The diffraction peaks at 28.3°, 32.8°, 47.2°, and 55.8° for the BWO, CBW, and CCBW samples can be assigned to the (131), (002), (202), and (331) planes of orthorhombic Bi2WO6 (JCPDS No. 39-0256) [14,15]. These similar patterns are attributed to the comparable substrate materials, which are consistent with the morphologies observed in TEM. Notably, the (101) plane at 28.3° of hexagonal CdS QDs (JCPDS No. 80-0006), also observed in the TEM images of the CBW and CCBW samples, overlaps with the (131) plane of Bi2WO6. Additionally, the (002) plane of CPDs is not observed in the CCBW patterns, likely due to the small quantity and weak diffraction signal of CPDs, as reported in previous studies [14].

Spectroscopic techniques were employed to elucidate the chemical composition and interactions within the CCBW-1 heterojunction. As shown in Fig. 2b, all samples exhibit two broad absorption bands in the infrared (IR) spectra, centered around 1560 and 3420 cm−1, which correspond to the OH vibrations of absorbed water. Pristine BWO displays characteristic peaks at 571, 719, and 1384 cm−1, attributed to the BiO, WO, and WOW stretching modes, respectively [66]. For CPDs, peaks at 1126, 1552, and 1654 cm−1 are associated with CNHC asymmetric stretching, NH bending, and CO stretching, respectively, confirming the presence of NH2 and COOH groups on their surface [67]. In the case of CBW, the WOW peak shows a blue shift compared to BWO, suggesting the disruption of WOW bonds. The characteristic CO peaks from CPDs in the ternary CCBW composites become broader with increasing CPDs content, likely indicating the interaction between CPDs and BWO [68]. Raman spectra were also employed to investigate the binding interactions of CdS QDs on BWO nanosheets, as shown in Fig. 2c. Notably, the OWO peak at ∼712 cm−1 in CBW, corresponding to the asymmetric Ag vibration mode [69], exhibits a redshift compared to bare BWO. This shift is likely due to the partial cleavage of the WO chain in tungstate and the formation of WOCd bonds at the interface between BWO and CdS QDs [63,65]. Additionally, a new peak appears around 255 cm−1 in CBW, near the peak at 305 cm−1, which is associated with the simultaneous motion of Bi3+ and [WO6]6−. This is similar to the BiS bond vibrations observed in Bi2S3 from our earlier study [63], suggesting the formation of new interfacial BiS bonds through electrostatic interactions.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to analyze the chemical states and composition of the samples, with detailed fitting parameters provided in Tables S1–S5. The adventitious C 1s peak at 284.80 eV was used as a reference to calculate the binding energies of the other elements. The survey spectra (Fig. S6) show the presence of Bi, W, O, Cd, S, and C elements in the CBW and CCBW-1 samples, while Cd and S are absent in the pristine BWO and CPBW samples, consistent with the EDS results in Fig. 1i. As shown in Fig. 2d, the Bi 4f XPS spectrum of BWO exhibits peaks at 164.62 and 159.32 eV, corresponding to the Bi 4f 5/2 and Bi 4f 7/2 states, respectively. The energy difference of 5.30 eV between these peaks is characteristic of Bi3+ within the crystal structure [14,15]. Similarly, the W 4f spectrum of BWO shows peaks at 37.72 and 35.54 eV for W 4f 5/2 and W 4f 7/2 states, respectively, confirming the presence of W6+ ions (Fig. 2e). The O 1s peaks at 532.42 and 530.01 eV (Fig. 2f) correspond to absorbed and lattice oxygen in BWO, respectively [15]. The Cd 3d peaks in the CdS QDs are observed at 411.75 and 405.04 eV, corresponding to the Cd 3d 3/2 and Cd 3d 5/2 states of Cd2+ ions (Fig. 2g) [39]. Notably, the Bi 4f, W 4f, and O 1s peaks of the CBW composite exhibit negative binding energy shifts relative to those of pristine BWO, whereas the Cd 3d and S 2p peaks show positive shifts compared to those of CdS QDs. This indicates electron transfer from CdS QDs to BWO at the heterointerface upon their contact. Specifically, the shoulder peak of O 1s shifts to lower energy, suggesting the formation of WOCd bonds (Fig. 2f). Given the higher electronegativity of O (3.5) compared to Cd (1.5) on the Allred-Rochow scale, electrons are expected to transfer from CdS QDs to BWO through the WOCd bonds across the heterointerface [65]. Additionally, the S 2p peak of CBW shifts toward higher binding energy compared to pristine BWO, likely due to the formation of interfacial BiS bonds. These results further support the strong interactions between BWO and CdS QDs, mediated by WOCd and BiS bonds, which enhance electron transfer at the heterointerface. This conclusion is consistent with the Raman spectroscopy results. For the CPBW sample, after the integration of CPDs and BWO, the C 1s peaks are observed at 288.76, 286.13, and 284.80 eV, corresponding to the CO, CN, and CC sp 2 hybrid bonds in CPDs, respectively (Fig. 2i) [70]. Furthermore, the Bi 4f, W 4f, and O 1s peaks of CPBW exhibit negative shifts compared to those of pristine BWO, indicating electron transfer from CPDs to Bi2WO6 at the CPDs/Bi2WO6 interface. Notably, an additional peak appears in the O 1s spectrum of CPBW at 533.79 eV compared to BWO and CBW, suggesting interactions between CPDs and BWO. Moreover, all corresponding peaks in CCBW-1 show negative shifts compared to the Bi 4f, W 4f, and O 1s peaks of CPBW, as well as the Bi 4f, W 4f, O 1s, Cd 3d, and S 2p peaks of CBW. This further confirms the transfer of free electrons from both CPDs and CdS QDs to BWO after the formation of the dual-QD heterostructure.

The optical properties of BWO, CBW, and CCBW-1 were investigated using UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS). As shown in Fig. 3 a, the sunlight absorption edge of the binary CBW sample extends from 431 nm to 557 nm compared to pristine BWO, attributed to the incorporation of CdS QDs, which exhibit strong visible-light absorption. This result demonstrates that the formation of a chemically bonded heterojunction can effectively broaden the light-harvesting range of the catalyst. Notably, the absorption edge of CCBW-1 shifts significantly to 623 nm, which is due to the prominent photosensitivity of CPDs (Fig. 3a). As confirmed in Fig. S7, the absorption range of the CCBW samples further expands with increasing CPDs content. The bandgaps (E g) of the catalysts were estimated using the Kubelka–Munk method, and the corresponding Tauc plots are presented in Fig. 3b [18]. CCBW-1 shows a smaller E g (1.80 eV) compared to BWO (2.57 eV), CdS QDs (2.30 eV), and CBW (2.03 eV). These results underscore that the synergistic combination of CdS QDs and CPDs in CCBW-1 enhances its visible-light absorption capability, which facilitates efficient photoenergy conversion and promotes photoinduced carrier excitation [71]. Additionally, the increased visible-light absorption is expected to enhance the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which can be involved in subsequent photoreactions, thereby improving the photocatalytic performance.

The charge transfer and separation characteristics within CCBW-1 were investigated using photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy and electrochemical methods. PL spectra were measured at room temperature with an excitation wavelength of 450 nm to assess the recombination behavior of photoinduced carriers. As shown in Fig. 3c, the PL peak around 700–850 nm in CBW almost disappears compared to pristine BWO after integration with CdS QDs. This reduction likely results from the formation of the heterostructure, which enhances interfacial charge transfer and separation [11]. Notably, CPDs exhibit a strong PL peak around 500–600 nm, which is significantly diminished in the ternary CCBW-1 sample. This decrease suggests enhanced carrier separation dynamics, likely due to the fluorescence quenching caused by the effective dispersion of CPDs across the CBW material [72]. The further reduction in PL intensity around 700–850 nm in CCBW-1 compared to BWO supports this interpretation. Additionally, the more intense PL signal of CCBW-1 at 500–600 nm relative to CBW can be attributed to the strong intrinsic luminescence of CPDs, consistent with previous reports [73,74]. Time-resolved PL measurements confirm the improved charge transfer property of CCBW-1 (Fig. 3d). The average carrier lifetimes (τ ave) of BWO, CBW, and CCBW-1 were calculated using Eq. (4) [75]:

with detailed data provided in Table S6. The results show that CCBW-1 exhibits a longer τ ave (4.57 ns) compared to BWO (1.21 ns) and CBW (2.17 ns). The increased τ ave reflects enhanced separation efficiency of e −–h + pairs [76]. To further investigate the efficient charge separation in CCBW-1, photocurrent response (i–t) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were also conducted.

To further understand the efficient charge separation in CCBW-1, photocurrent response (i–t) and EIS measurements were conducted. As shown in Fig. 3e, the photocurrent intensity of CCBW-1 is higher than that of both binary CBW and pristine BWO, indicating an increased concentration of interfacial carriers due to enhanced charge separation. Additionally, the Nyquist plots in Fig. 3f reveal that CCBW-1 exhibits a smaller arc radius compared to CBW and BWO, suggesting lower charge transfer resistance (R ct) and improved interfacial charge transfer efficiency [77]. These results confirm that the chemically bonded interface, formed by BiS and WOC bonds within the heterojunction, not only enhances stability but also serves as an efficient pathway for rapid electron transfer at the atomic level. In summary, these analyses demonstrate that the CCBW-1 heterojunction excels in visible-light absorption and efficiently generates, separates, and transfers charge carriers to the reactive sites, leading to enhanced photoreaction efficiency.

Photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction experiments were conducted to assess the heavy metal ion removal efficiency of the samples, as shown in Fig. 4 a, b. Based on the adsorption–desorption equilibrium of various photocatalysts (Fig. S10), a dark reaction time of 30 min was selected. Blank experiments confirmed that Cr(VI) photoreduction was negligible without catalysts. The Cr(VI) photoreduction efficiency (excluding adsorption) of the CBW sample increased to 49.4 % after 30 min of LED irradiation, compared to 34.7 % for the pristine BWO sample. The photocatalytic reduction rate constant k for CBW (0.040 min−1) was approximately 1.6 times higher than that of the pristine BWO sample (0.025 min−1), indicating that the strong interfacial interactions between BWO and CdS QDs enhanced the transfer and separation of charge carriers via the IEF. Upon incorporating CPDs, the optimized CCBW-1 sample exhibited excellent photoreduction activity, achieving a Cr(VI) removal of up to 97.7 % (including adsorption) and a photoreduction efficiency of 71.9 % within 30 min. The corresponding photoreduction rate constant (0.111 min−1) was approximately 4.4, 2.8, and 10.1 times higher than those of pristine BWO, CBW, and CPBW (0.011 min−1), respectively. The superior photocatalytic performance of CCBW-1 can be attributed to the formation of a rational dual-QD heterostructure, enabling BWO to leverage the broad-spectrum light absorption and deep reduction potential of both QDs, resulting in a synergistic enhancement effect. Moreover, the degree of enhancement in photocatalytic performance suggests that the contribution of the CdS QD heterojunction was more significant than that of the CPDs, likely due to the higher content of CdS QDs and the well-designed Z-scheme heterojunction. The introduction of both types of QDs allows Bi2WO6 to simultaneously harness broad-spectrum light absorption and deep reduction potentials [54].

Apart from high performance, the reusability of photocatalysts is a crucial factor for their practical application. Therefore, cycling experiments were conducted to assess the stability of the CCBW-1 sample. As shown in Fig. 4c, CCBW-1 demonstrates excellent Cr(VI) photoreduction performance and stability after four cycles, outperforming pristine BWO in terms of sustained efficiency. Moreover, XRD (Fig. S11a), IR (Fig. S11b), and XPS survey spectra (Fig. S11c), as well as corresponding high-resolution profiles (Fig. S12), reveal minimal changes before and after cycling, confirming the exceptional stability of CCBW-1. Notably, the XPS spectra of the used CCBW-1 samples show new peaks at 586.80 and 576.85 eV, corresponding to Cr3+ ions, which confirms the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) during photocatalysis (Fig. 4d). This finding is consistent with previous reports [78].

The photocatalytic efficiency of CCBW-1 for Cr(VI) removal was evaluated at various pH levels. As shown in Fig. 4e, CCBW-1 achieves Cr(VI) reduction efficiencies of 98.7 %, 94.8 %, 94.6 %, 79.9 %, and 65.2 % at pH values of 2.5, 4.0, ∼5.5 (natural), 7.0, and 8.5, respectively, after 20 min of irradiation. Notably, CCBW-1 demonstrates the highest Cr(VI) reduction under acidic conditions, suggesting that higher concentrations of H+ ions promote the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III). Conversely, the reduced activity under alkaline conditions can be attributed to the limited availability of H+ ions for the reduction process [79].

To evaluate the practical application potential of CCBW-1, photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction was tested using real water samples. River water from Guanshan Reservoir in Kunming, China (24.82449°N, 102.82988°E), and tap water from Kunming Water Supply Group Co., Ltd. (China), were used to prepare Cr(VI) solutions. Under the same experimental conditions, the Cr(VI) reduction efficiency of CCBW-1 decreased to 86.7 % in river water, while it remained high at 96.1 % in tap water (Fig. 4f). This decrease in efficiency in river water is likely due to the presence of various organic and inorganic impurities, which may hinder light absorption and block active sites [19].

To simulate more complex aqueous environments, the Cr(VI) reduction ability of CCBW-1 in the presence of various anions and cations was also assessed (Fig. 4g, h). The results show that CCBW-1 exhibits significant resistance to ionic interference at 1 mmol/L concentration. Among the anions tested, the inhibition followed the order: CO3 2− (74.9 %) > SO4 2− (82.6 %) ≈ Cl− (83.7 %) > NO3 − (86.6 %), with CO3 2− causing the most inhibition, likely due to the pH increase from the formation of HCO3 − and OH− [79]. In the case of cations, the order of inhibition was Ca2+ > K+ > Mg2+ ≈ Na+, although the effects were minimal.

To assess the photocatalytic performance of CCBW-1 under different atmospheric conditions, experiments were conducted in N2, air, and O2. As shown in Fig. 4i, Cr(VI) reduction efficiencies after 15 min of illumination were 62.9 %, 90.0 %, and 96.4 % under N2, air, and O2 atmospheres, respectively. The higher performance under air and O2 atmospheres can be attributed to the presence of dissolved O2, which reacts with e − to produce superoxide radicals (O2 −), further facilitating Cr(VI) reduction [71,80]. Thus, an O2-rich atmosphere significantly enhances the photocatalytic performance of CCBW-1. Notably, the reduction efficiency reaches a high level after 20 min of illumination under both air and O2 conditions.

To evaluate the photooxidation performance of CCBW-1, RhB degradation experiments were conducted. As shown in Fig. S14, CCBW-1 achieves an impressive RhB removal efficiency of 99.8 % within 30 min, with a rate constant (0.122 min−1) approximately 20.3 and 1.7 times higher than those of BWO (0.006 min−1) and CBW (0.071 min−1), respectively, indicating its superior oxidation capacity. Interestingly, the RhB degradation efficiency of the ternary CCBW heterojunctions increases initially with CPDs content but then decreases with excessive CPDs. This behavior can be attributed to the fact that an excess of CPDs may hinder the interfacial diffusion and utilization of photoinduced h + in the valence bands (VB) of BWO, which is responsible for strong oxidation [15]. The mineralization ability of CCBW-1 was further evaluated by measuring the total organic carbon (TOC) concentration in RhB wastewater before and after photocatalytic degradation. After 40 min of irradiation, CCBW-1 achieved a TOC removal efficiency of 41.3 %, demonstrating its excellent mineralization capacity.

To assess the photocatalytic performance of CCBW-1 in the presence of mixed pollutants, experiments were conducted using a solution containing both Cr(VI) and RhB, with a total pollutant concentration of 40 mg/L. As shown in Fig. 5 b, d, at a Cr(VI)/RhB concentration ratio of 1:1, the Cr(VI) reduction rate constant (0.102 min−1) is 1.34 times greater than in the single Cr(VI) system, while the RhB degradation rate constant (0.120 min−1) is 1.24 times higher than in the pure RhB system. This enhancement in performance can be attributed to the distinct active species driving each process. Specifically, e − primarily facilitate Cr(VI) reduction, while h + are crucial for RhB degradation [81,82]. When Cr(VI) and RhB concentrations are equal, the reduction of Cr(VI) depletes e −, which in turn promotes the generation and migration of h +, enhancing RhB degradation. This results in a synergistic enhancement of photocatalytic performance. However, when the concentrations of the two pollutants are different, the imbalance in e − (or h +) generation can inhibit the other species’ activity, leading to lower efficiency compared to single-pollutant systems. This is evident from the rate constants for Cr(VI)/RhB ratios of 2:3 or 3:2, which are smaller than those for pure Cr(VI) or RhB, supporting the above explanation. Overall, CCBW-1 demonstrates excellent potential as a photocatalyst for the simultaneous removal of heavy metals and organic contaminants. The Cr(VI) and RhB removal efficiencies achieved by the CCBW-1 system, using only a 10 W LED light source, are competitive with those reported in recent literature (Table S8), highlighting its promise for water treatment and environmental remediation.

To investigate the roles of active species in the photocatalytic removal of Cr(VI) and RhB, various radical scavengers were used to capture e −, h +, hydroxyl radicals (OH), and O2 −. These included AgNO3 e − scavenger), EDTA-2Na (h + scavenger), isopropanol (IPA, OH scavenger), and benzoquinone (PBQ, O2 − scavenger) [83]. To eliminate the influence of Ag+ reduction, K2S2O8 was used as an e − quencher during Cr(VI) reduction [75]. As shown in Fig. S16a, b, the addition of IPA or EDTA-2Na caused no significant reduction in Cr(VI) removal efficiency. However, the introduction of K2S2O8 and PBQ led to a marked decrease in the Cr(VI) reduction rate constant by 65.1 % and 37.4 %, respectively. This indicates that e − are the primary active species in Cr(VI) reduction, with O2 − also playing a role, which is further supported by the results in Fig. 4i. For RhB degradation, as shown in Fig. S16c, d, the addition of AgNO3 or PBQ had little effect, while EDTA-2Na and IPA significantly reduced RhB removal efficiency by 86.8 % and 75.8 %, respectively. This suggests that h + are the dominant active species in RhB degradation, with a secondary contribution from OH.

To further confirm the active species involved, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was conducted using TEMPOL, TEMPO, and DMPO as spin-trapping agents to detect e −, h +, OH, and O2 − signals under light illumination. As shown in Fig. 6 a, no ESR signals for TEMPOL−e −, DMPO−OH, or DMPO−O2 − were observed in the dark, but these signals were significantly enhanced after 10 min of light exposure, confirming the generation of e −, OH, and O2 − (Fig. 6a, b). Conversely, the TEMPO−h + signal, which was present in the dark, almost disappeared upon light irradiation, confirming the generation of h +. These results align with quenching experiments and confirm the production of all four active species in CCBW-1. The strong OH and O2 − signals suggest that the accumulation rates of e − and h + are much higher than their consumption rates, indicating efficient photogenerated charge separation and migration in the CCBW-1 photocatalyst.

Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) and Mott–Schottky analysis were employed to determine the work functions (Φ), energy band positions, and semiconductor types of the CCBW-1 heterojunction. As shown in Fig. 6c, the Φ values for BWO and CdS QDs were determined to be 5.75 and 4.55 eV, respectively, using UPS spectra and Eq. (5),
(5)

where hv is the incident photon energy (21.22 eV), and the cutoff energies (E cutoff) for BWO and CdS QDs were 15.48 and 16.67 eV, respectively. The onset potentials (E onset) of the two materials were 1.32 eV (BWO) and 1.70 eV (CdS QDs) (Fig. 6d), giving the corresponding valence band potentials (E VB) of 7.06 eV and 6.25 eV (vs. E vac), which are 2.62 eV and 1.81 eV relative to the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) (see Table S7). Based on the bandgaps of 2.57 eV (BWO) and 2.30 eV (CdS QDs) from DRS, the conduction band potentials (E CB) of BWO and CdS QDs were calculated to be 0.05 eV and −0.49 eV, respectively. Mott–Schottky curves (Fig. 6e–g) indicated the flat band potentials (E fb) for BWO, CdS QDs, and CPDs to be −0.40 eV, −0.92 eV, and −0.96 eV (vs. Ag/AgCl, pH ≈ 6.8), respectively. The positive slopes of these curves confirmed the n-type semiconductor nature of all the materials. Using Eq. (6) , these potentials relative to NHE were calculated to be 0.20 eV, −0.32 eV, and −0.36 eV, respectively.

Generally, the E CB positions of n-type semiconductors are approximately 0.2 eV lower than their E fb values, resulting in E CB values for BWO, CdS QDs, and CPDs of 0.0 eV, −0.52 eV, and −0.56 eV (vs. NHE, pH = 7), respectively [84]. Based on this, the E VB positions of BWO and CdS QDs were determined to be 2.57 eV and 1.78 eV, respectively. These band positions from UPS align closely with those obtained from the Mott–Schottky results.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were further employed to deeper understand the charge transfer mechanism and bonding characteristics at the heterointerfaces. The computed Φ values for BWO, (CdS)4 clusters, and CPDs were 5.67 eV, 4.32 eV, and 4.54 eV, respectively, with corresponding Fermi levels (E f) of −7.37 eV, −4.75 eV, and −4.10 eV (Fig. 7 a–c). These results align with the trends observed in the UPS data. Additionally, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energies of CPDs were found to be −0.72 eV and 1.21 eV (vs. NHE), respectively, yielding a bandgap of 1.93 eV (Fig. 7d). The LUMO value approximates the E CB (−0.56 eV) obtained from Mott–Schottky analysis. Notably, the E CB and E VB values for the CdS QDs/Bi2WO6 and CPDs/Bi2WO6 systems meet the thermodynamic requirements for the generation of O2 − and OH radicals, respectively. The electrostatic potential distribution of CPDs (Fig. 8 d) reveals that regions around the NH2 and OH groups exhibit positive electrostatic potential values, while those near the COOH groups display negative values. This distribution suggests that electrons accumulate around the NH2 and OH groups, which act as electron donors, while the COOH group acts as an electron acceptor during the photoreaction process. The charge density difference calculations along the Z-direction in the CBW and CPBW heterojunctions further examined the interfacial electron transport in the CCBW-1 sample. The optimal structure (Fig. 7e) reveals that the heterointerface between BWO and (CdS)4 clusters is constructed via BiS and WOCd bonds, consistent with spectroscopic analyses. In contrast, the binding between BWO and CPDs primarily involves hydrogen bonds (OH…O = 2.75 Å) (Fig. 7f). Interestingly, electrons accumulate predominantly on the BWO side in both CBW and CPBW heterojunctions, suggesting that electrons transfer from CdS QDs and CPDs to BWO. This result is in agreement with work function analyses and XPS results. Furthermore, the significant increase in charge density on the BWO side of the CPBW heterojunction indicates enhanced interfacial interaction, attributed to the electron-donor effect of CPDs, further reinforcing the structural stability of CCBW-1. In summary, the outstanding photocatalytic performance and stability of CCBW-1 can be attributed to the synergistic effect of the intimate charge transfer channel between BWO and CdS QDs, along with the electron-donor properties of CPDs, particularly the NH2 and OH groups. This synergy enables efficient and stable separation of charge carriers.

The interfacial bonds in the CdS QDs/Bi2WO6 heterojunction were further confirmed through 2D mapping of the electronic location function (ELF) and projected density of states (PDOS) analysis [71,85]. After integrating the CdS QDs with BWO, higher ELF values were observed in regions associated with W, O, and Cd atoms, as well as Bi and S atoms (Fig. 8a, b). This indicates stronger electronic localization around these atoms, suggesting the formation of relatively strong WOCd and BiS bonds via electrostatic interactions. A similar observation was made from the 2D slices of charge density difference (EDD) (Fig. S17). These bonds likely serve as atomic-level pathways, facilitating efficient electron migration and transfer at the heterointerface. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 8c, d, the PDOS diagrams reveal substantial orbital overlap between the electronic occupied states of W 5d, O 2p, and Cd 4d, as well as between Bi 6s, Bi 6p, and S 2p at the heterointerface, further confirming the formation of these interfacial bonds. Notably, relative to pristine CdS QDs (Fig. S18b), the Cd 4d state shifts closer to the Fermi level in the heterojunction (Fig. 8c), indicating that the integration with BWO enhances the bonding ability of the CdS QDs and allows more electrons to be utilized [86]. Additionally, the S 2s state nearly disappears in the heterojunction (Fig. 8d) compared to its presence in CdS QDs (Fig. S18b), suggesting that free electrons flow from CdS QDs to the BWO. This observation is consistent with the XPS and work function analyses.

Based on both experimental and computational results, a reasonable photocatalytic mechanism for Cr(VI) and RhB removal on the CCBW-1 heterojunction is proposed. Given that the work function (Φ) values of CdS QDs and CPDs are smaller than that of BWO, while the Fermi levels (E f) of CdS QDs and CPDs are higher than that of BWO (Fig. 9 a, b and Table 1 ), free electrons from CdS QDs and CPDs will preferentially migrate to BWO across the heterointerfaces. At the heterointerface, this electron transfer causes the BWO side to become negatively charged, while the CdS QDs and CPDs sides become positively charged, thereby generating an IEF directed from the CdS QDs and CPDs sides to the BWO side (Fig. 9b). This leads to band bending until Fermi levels equilibrate, establishing a gradient of E f from the contact area outward [65].

Under LED light irradiation, e − in the VB of all three components are promoted to their respective conduction bands (CB), leaving behind h+ in the VB (Fig. 9c). Based on the energy band structures, two possible charge transfer mechanisms can occur: II-scheme or Z-scheme. In the case of the Z-scheme, the IEF drives photoexcited e − in the CB of BWO to migrate in opposite directions, where they recombine with h + in the VB of CdS QDs and CPDs at the heterointerfaces. This mechanism retains e − in the CB of CdS QDs and CPDs, which have a stronger reduction potential, while the h + in the VB of BWO remain with a stronger oxidation potential. These separated charge carriers then migrate to the catalyst surface, where they participate in the reactions. Consequently, the CCBW-1 heterostructure achieves efficient separation of e −–h + pairs, promoting simultaneous Cr(VI) reduction and RhB oxidation half-reactions. Conversely, under the II-scheme pathway, the IEF would drive e − in the CB of CdS QDs and CPDs to transfer to the CB of BWO, while h + in the VB of BWO would transfer to the VB of CdS QDs and CPDs. However, this hypothesis faces limitations. The CB potential of BWO (0.05 eV) is more positive than the redox potential of O2/O2 − (−0.33 eV), preventing dissolved O2 from undergoing reduction. Similarly, the VB potentials of CdS QDs and CPDs (1.81 eV and 1.21 eV, respectively) are more negative than the redox potential of OH−/OH (1.99 eV vs. NHE), meaning OH radicals cannot form. These speculative outcomes from the II-scheme pathway are inconsistent with the observed ESR spectra, further supporting the idea that the dual Z-scheme mechanism more accurately explains the photocatalytic removal of Cr(VI) and RhB.

To investigate the influence of CPDs’ surface structures on interfacial electron properties, calculations were performed on CPDs with various functional groups and their corresponding CPDs/Bi2WO6 heterojunctions. As shown in Fig. 10 a, the positive electrostatic potential maps indicate that CPDs with OH and NH2 groups exhibit stronger electron-donating capabilities due to their higher electron density. These functional groups enhance the IEF, thereby promoting more efficient charge transfer at the CPDs/Bi2WO6 heterointerface. The LUMO energy levels of the CPDs show that CPDs with NH2 groups have the most negative LUMO energy level (−1.52 eV), followed by CPDs with OH groups (−0.51 eV), while CPDs with COOH groups display the most positive LUMO level (0.0 eV). This suggests that introducing NH2 groups can effectively shift the energy levels of CPDs toward more negative values, which is beneficial for enhancing the Cr(VI) photoreduction performance of the hybrid materials. The work functions, Mulliken charge transfer values, interfacial interaction energies (E int), and adhesion works (W ad) of the CPDs/Bi2WO6 heterojunctions were calculated (Table 2 ). The work functions decrease in the following order: COOH > H > OH > NH2, whereas the charge transfer values increase in the same order. CPDs with electron-donating groups such as NH2 and OH show larger electron transfer values (0.52 and 0.30 e −, respectively) compared to electron-withdrawing groups such as COOH. These electron-donating groups reduce the work function, promote electron delocalization, and enhance electron supply efficiency. Additionally, all interaction energies were negative, and the adhesion works were positive, indicating excellent structural stability for the CPDs/Bi2WO6 heterojunctions. The stability of these systems follows the order: NH2 >OH > H ≈ COOH. Charge density differences along the Z-axis for various functional groups show consistent charge transfer directions, with charge carriers migrating from CPDs to BWO at the heterointerfaces (Fig. 10b–e). Notably, after structural optimization, only the H atom of the NH2 group on NH2-modified CPDs forms a hydrogen bond with the O atom of the [WO4]2− layer on the Bi2WO6 surface (NH…O = 3.13 Å) (Fig. 10c). This results in a significant decrease in the electron density on the N atoms of the CPD surface, reinforcing electron delocalization on the H atom and facilitating hydrogen bond formation. Furthermore, average charge density difference curves reveal that CPDs dominated by NH2 and OH groups exhibit smaller charge loss values (−0.0072 and −0.0082 e/Å, respectively) compared to those with COOH groups (−0.0106 e/Å). These results confirm the higher electron storage capacities of CPDs with NH2 and OH groups and emphasize the crucial role of electron-donating groups in promoting charge transfer. In conclusion, the heterojunction formed by the integration of CPDs enriched with NH2 and OH groups into BWO exhibits improved reduction capacity, enhanced charge interfacial transfer properties, and greater structural stability. This improvement can be attributed to the deeper conduction band and superior electron storage capacity and conductivity of these CPDs.

The intermediates generated during the photocatalytic degradation of RhB were identified using liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (LC–MS), and the degradation pathways were proposed. LC-MS data (Fig. S19) revealed several degradation products with prominent peaks at mass-to-charge ratios (m/z) of 443, 415, 387, 365, 359, 331, 215, 207, 164, 114, 84, 82, and 72. These products were assigned as P1 to P15, respectively (Table S9). Based on these findings, a plausible photocatalytic RhB degradation pathway for CCBW-1 was proposed (Fig. 11 a). Initially, the ethyl groups of RhB (m/z = 443) undergo degradation to form intermediates such as P2 (415), P3 (387), P5 (359), and P6 (331). Subsequently, hydroxylation and deamination reactions occur under the attack of ROS, yielding smaller intermediates, including P4 (365), P7 (215), P8 (207), P9 (164), and P10 (164). These intermediates then undergo ring-opening reactions, breaking the aromatic rings into low-molecular-weight organic compounds. Finally, these smaller molecules are further oxidized and mineralized into CO2 and H2O.

The ecological toxicity of RhB and its degradation products in the aqueous environment was predicted using the T.E.S.T. software package (Fig. 11b–e and Table S10). Fig. 11b shows that the LC50 value for Daphnia magna is significantly higher for the smaller molecular products (P11–P15) compared to pristine RhB. Similarly, the LC50 value for Fathead minnow also increases for these intermediates compared to RhB (Fig. 11c). These results indicate a notable reduction in biological toxicity following the photocatalytic degradation. Moreover, the bioaccumulation factors for most intermediates (P9–P15) are substantially lower than those for RhB (Fig. 11d). Additionally, the developmental toxicity of the intermediates (P9–P15) decreases as photodegradation progresses (Fig. 11e), with the stable product P13 exhibiting the lowest developmental toxicity. Overall, the CCBW-1 photocatalytic system effectively mitigates the toxicity of RhB and its degradation products.

The detailed process of photocatalytic simultaneous Cr(VI) and RhB elimination was elucidated based on the proposed Z-scheme mechanism, as shown in Fig. 12 . The e − in the CB of CdS QDs and CPDs can either directly reduce Cr(VI) to Cr(III) or react with dissolved oxygen to generate O2 − radicals, which subsequently interact with Cr(VI). RhB molecules are oxidized by h + and the ROS generated, primarily OH radicals. The key reactions involved can be described by Eqs. (7)–(12). In summary, the synergetic effects of the Z-scheme electron transfer mechanism, dual QDs with deep reduction potentials, and enhanced interfacial interactions significantly improve the generation and utilization of photoexcited carriers, leading to the simultaneous removal of heavy metal ions and organic pollutants.

m o l e c u l e s

In this study, a dual-quantum dot heterojunction (CCBW) consisting of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), CdS quantum dots (QDs), and Bi2WO6 was successfully synthesized using a microwave-assisted solvothermal method. The enhanced photocatalytic performance of the CCBW can be attributed to its dual Z-scheme charge migration pathway and the quantum size effects of CPDs and CdS QDs, which facilitate the spatial separation and migration of photoinduced carriers. These effects extend the visible-light absorption range, improve the redox capabilities, and generate abundant ROS. Importantly, the BiS and WOCd bonds formed at the heterointerfaces act as atomic-level channels for efficient electron transfer from CdS QDs to Bi2WO6. The electron-donating effect of CPDs, particularly those functionalized with NH2 and OH groups, further promotes interfacial charge transfer. As a result, the CCBW-1 photocatalyst demonstrated a 97.7 % removal efficiency of Cr(VI) within 30 min under 10 W LED irradiation, with reaction rate constants that are 4.4, 2.8, and 10.1 times higher than those of pristine Bi2WO6, CdS QDs/Bi2WO6, and 3 % CPDs/Bi2WO6, respectively. Notably, 96.9 % of Cr(VI) and 98.1 % of Rhodamine B (RhB) were simultaneously removed within 30 min in a mixed Cr(VI) and RhB solution. Additionally, the CCBW-1 heterostructure exhibited excellent reusability and resistance to interference. Toxicology assessments confirmed that the CCBW-1 system effectively reduced the toxicity of RhB degradation products. This work not only provides a novel approach to the fabrication of dual-QD heterostructures but also emphasizes the importance of CPDs-mediated interfacial bonding in the development of advanced photocatalysts for environmental remediation.
